turns.student: How have you been feeling overall lately?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been pretty anxious and worried about my health. The past few weeks have been tough with these... episodes of diarrhea. It's really affecting my work performance and just overall quality of life.

turns.student: Tell me about your day-to-day — how has your health been in general?
turns.patient: It's been pretty debilitating, to be honest. I've had these crampy lower abdominal pains, mainly on my left side, and they're getting worse with each episode of diarrhea. The bloody stools have been really concerning me - it's always a mix of bright red blood and mucus, and it happens after almost every bowel movement. And the frequency... I'm talking 8 to 10 times a day now, which is completely off from my usual routine. It's like my body is just constantly in distress.

turns.student: You mentioned some health issues. Can you walk me through what happens?
turns.patient: It usually starts with these crampy pains in my lower abdomen, mostly on the left side. They can be pretty severe and come on suddenly. Then, I'll feel this urge to go to the bathroom, and when I do, it's like a constant stream of diarrhea - loose to watery, and it's always urgent. Sometimes, after I've gone, there will be blood mixed with mucus in my stool, which is really scary for me because I don't know what it means or why it's happening.

turns.student: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
turns.patient: When these episodes happen, my body feels like it's under attack - everything gets tight and crampy down there. I feel this intense urge to go to the bathroom right away, and even after I've had a bowel movement, the pain doesn't really go away; it just kind of... lingers, making me feel anxious and uncomfortable for hours afterwards.

turns.student: Let's focus on the episodes. Walk me through one from start to finish.
turns.patient: It starts with this crampy pain in my lower abdomen, usually on the left side, which can come on suddenly and feels really intense. Then, I'll feel a strong urge to have a bowel movement, and when I do, it's like a constant stream of watery diarrhea - it's loose and urgent, and I have to rush to the bathroom. Sometimes, right after I've gone, I'll notice blood mixed with mucus in my stool, which is really worrying for me. The pain doesn't fully go away even after I've had a bowel movement; it just kind of... lingers, leaving me feeling anxious and uncomfortable for hours afterwards.

turns.student: During an episode, do you notice anything besides the main symptom?
turns.patient: Yes, besides the diarrhea, I also feel fatigued, really tired, like my body is just exhausted from it all. And sometimes, during or right after an episode, I'll feel a bit lightheaded or dizzy, which can be scary because I'm not sure what's causing it.

turns.student: Specifically, any changes in your skin — like sweating or flushing?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't really noticed anything out of the ordinary with my skin during these episodes. I mean, I've been feeling hot and flushed before, but that could be just from the anxiety and stress of dealing with this. And sometimes, after an episode, I might feel a bit clammy or sweaty, but I wouldn't say it's a consistent thing.

turns.student: And your stomach — any queasiness or nausea during these moments?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah, there have been times when I've felt nauseous after an episode. It's not always the case, but sometimes, especially if the diarrhea has been particularly bad, I'll feel a bit queasy or lightheaded afterwards. Like, my stomach will just kind of... churn or feel upset for a while, which is really uncomfortable and makes me worry even more about what's going on with my body.

turns.student: What about other parts of your body? Any pain, tingling, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: Honestly, no. The crampy pains are mostly in my lower abdomen, and I don't feel any pain or discomfort in my arms, legs, chest, or head during these episodes. It's really just that one area down there that feels like it's under attack.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: I've actually noticed that I get a low-grade fever, around 37.8 to 38.0 degrees Celsius, a few times per week. It's not always consistent, but it seems like when these episodes of diarrhea are happening more frequently, my body temperature tends to spike slightly.

turns.student: Now let's zoom out. Any swelling you've noticed anywhere on your body?
turns.patient: Honestly, no, I haven't noticed any significant swelling anywhere on my body. But to be honest, with all these episodes of diarrhea, I'm more concerned about what might be causing it than physical symptoms like swelling. The weight loss has been a bigger concern for me lately - I've lost around 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past three weeks, and that's really affecting my mood and energy levels.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your weight or appetite?
turns.patient: Yes, actually. With these episodes of diarrhea, I've been having trouble keeping food down for a while after they happen, so I haven't been eating as much as usual. And even when I do try to eat, it's mostly takeout because that's just easier with my schedule. But overall, I think the weight loss is a bigger concern for me - 4 kg in three weeks is a lot, and it's really affecting my mood and energy levels.

turns.student: Let me ask about your medical history. What conditions have you been treated for?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't had any significant prior medical conditions that I'm aware of. I did have an appendectomy at age 16, but that was a routine surgery and didn't cause me any long-term issues. Other than that, I've been relatively healthy throughout my life. I don't take any regular medications or supplements unless it's for something minor like ibuprofen for headaches.

turns.student: Any medications you're taking? Are you taking them consistently?
turns.patient: Actually, I have been taking loperamide, also known as Imodium, off and on for the past two weeks to try and manage these episodes of diarrhea. I've been using it when I feel an episode coming on or after it's happened, trying to get some relief from the symptoms. But even with that, the relief is minimal, and I'm starting to get really worried about what's causing this in the first place.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle. How active are you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my work schedule has been really demanding lately - 60+ hour weeks as a software engineer at a startup can be pretty grueling. I've been trying to stay active by taking short walks around my apartment complex during breaks, but it's not the same as having consistent exercise or a regular routine. With these episodes of diarrhea, even simple tasks feel exhausting, so I'm not getting much physical activity beyond that.

turns.student: And your diet — what does a typical day of eating look like?
turns.patient: My diet has been really irregular lately due to my work schedule. I've been mostly surviving on takeout and whatever is convenient, which isn't ideal at all. I'm not even eating three meals a day consistently; it's more like grabbing something whenever I can between meetings or deadlines. It's not healthy, and I know that's probably contributing to some of these issues, but it's just been so hard to find time for cooking or meal prep.

turns.student: How about alcohol or smoking?
turns.patient: I do drink a bit - usually 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month, I've found myself increasing that to 4-5 beers per night due to stress from these symptoms and work. As for smoking, I'm definitely not a smoker.

turns.student: Now family history. Any medical problems run in your family?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. She's been dealing with it ever since. It's been pretty tough on our whole family, to be honest - the uncertainty and unpredictability of her condition have made us all a bit more anxious about health issues in general.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious or chronic conditions?
turns.patient: Yeah... my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been pretty tough on our whole family - dealing with her condition and all the unpredictability that comes with it. We're all a bit more anxious about health issues in general, to be honest.

turns.student: We've covered a lot. Is there anything we haven't discussed that you think is relevant?
turns.patient: To be honest, I think we've covered most of what's been going on with my symptoms and some background information about my health. One thing that might be worth mentioning is that I did travel to Mexico for a work conference about three months ago, but I didn't have any GI issues during or after the trip. And as far as family history goes, my father has type 2 diabetes, which makes me think maybe there's some genetic component to this whole thing... but I'm not sure if that's relevant right now.
